text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic Heller's myotomy, opening the peritoneum over the pars flaccida above the caudate lobe of the liver using a harmonic device. The peritoneum is lifted for anterior mobilization of the esophagus, revealing the vena cava below the pars flaccida and caudate lobe, with the right crest visible.

The surgeon stretches the peritoneum downward to prevent injury to the anterior vagus nerve, aiming to achieve a proper fascial plane towards the angle of His at the left gastroesophageal junction. The spleen is visible on the extreme left. Further dissection is performed, and the esophagus is visible as the peritoneum over the right crus is dissected.
The surgeon holds and stretches the fundus of the stomach downward to achieve a proper peritoneal plane. The peritoneum over the left crus is visibly separated.

The surgeon cuts at least five centimeters of the muscular layer of the esophagus, both circularly and longitudinally, to achieve the desired effect of the Heller's myotomy. The liver retractor is rearranged to improve exposure for the mobilization of the esophagus, with the right crus visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon begins posterior mobilization of the esophagus, separating it from the right crest. A small gauze piece is used to manage minor oozing and assist in blunt dissection. The muscle is stretched using an atraumatic grasper and Maryland dissector, allowing separation of longitudinal fibers until the mucosa bulges visibly. Care is taken to avoid perforation of the esophagus, and minimal energy use is employed to control oozing.

The surgeon observes the gastroesophageal junction, noting the circular muscle fibers joining at this level. Due to the patient's good intra-abdominal length of the esophagus, extensive mobilization is not required.

The surgeon continues slitting the muscle fibers of the esophagus to expose the underlying mucosa, ensuring that residual fibers are carefully managed. Pressure is applied to control any bleeding observed during the procedure.

The surgeon begins suturing the left side of the esophageal muscle using 3-0 or 2-0 Ethibond sutures. The left split longitudinal muscle of the esophagus is sutured, with intracorporeal techniques applied. Scissors are used from the left side to trim the suture tails after knotting.

The surgeon uses a Nathanson liver retractor to lift and adjust the liver for optimal exposure. An intracorporeal suture is introduced, limited to twenty centimeters in length. The omentum is trapped by the left instrument, requiring careful handling. The surgeon proceeds with the anterior partial wrap, known as the Dor fundoplication, ensuring the right crus is included without damaging the vena cava. The anterior portion is pulled with a grasper, and the knot is tightened using a sudden knot technique due to limited traction.

The surgeon takes a bite through the right side of the esophageal muscle and the right crus using a Maryland knot and an assisting needle holder. Care is taken to ensure the wrap does not get trapped in the joints of the Maryland instrument before tying the knot.

The surgeon tightens the last knot in the suturing process, carefully removes the needle, and ensures the gauze piece is taken out. The lever is gradually lowered, completing the laparoscopic helix myotomy procedure.
